W A	S	T	E
T	I	ME

D	a	nK	e	n	n	e	d	yA	r	c	h	i	v	e	s
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 16 --

REPRINTED FROM
DAN S. KENNEDY’S No B.S. Information Marketing Letter
FEBRUARY, 2010
Each Special Report brings you an extended length dissertation on one aspect of info-
marketing. These Reports alternate every other month with Issues of The No B.S.
INFO-Marketing Letter*; 6 times a year, a new Report, 6 times a year, the newsletter.
The Letter/Reports are included in Information Marketing Association Membership as a
Member Benefit (www.info-marketing.org).
*NOT to be confused with the regular No B.S. Marketing Letter or The No B.S. Marketing To The Affluent
Letter also published by GKIC.)
© 2014/GKIC
*********************************************************************************
The Information Marketing Association provides Insider Secrets, Access to Experts and Step-by-Step
Resources Assembled for Creating and Running Simple and Easy Multimillion-Dollar Information
Marketing Businesses
Successful authors, self-publishers of books, people who sell home study courses, personal and
business coaches, newsletter publishers, and seminar promoters turn to the Information Marketing
Association for the critical insights on the business side of running a successful information marketing
business. This information gap can only be filled by the one and only trade association for the
information marketing industry.
The IMA offers everything you need to continue to grow your information marketing business under
one umbrella. The benefits are too many to list.
Because you have this report, you can experience all of the benefits for free, for 2-months, with an
invitation only opportunity to get a two month trial membership. Just visit www.IMAFree.com to take
advantage of this opportunity, while it lasts.
*********************************************************************************
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 16 --

Hardly anybody matches my personal productivity – because they refuse to embrace my
methods (most of which are not at all secret). There is a growing „revolt against the
machine‟ – people disconnecting, taking back their time. And a growing recognition that the
constant connectedness is more harmful than helpful – two articles about this, reprinted
here, and I recently saw a Frontline program on PBS featuring a Stanford scientist‟s
research using MRIs to demonstrate potential permanent damage to the very ability to
think from too much computer, too little reading… and speculation by other experts that
we may be fundamentally altering what it means to be a human being by our enslavement
to the simultaneous connectedness and isolation of the digital world, and the constant
disruption of thought and work. (Incidentally, the average pre-teen now spends 54 hours
every week immersed in digital i.e. web sites, texting, games, etc. – 14 hours more than a
full-time work week.) But this is
not a Report about my personal disdain for people who
have become willing slaves to the constant connectedness, entertainment, and diversion
provided by technology run amok. This is about the bigger, broader subject of how your
time is invested.
Most information-entrepreneurs are neither blessed nor burdened with large staffs,
themselves wear multiple hats, and are
the driving force of their businesses. It is probably
true that your income has more direct connection to how effectively your time is invested
than for the owners or proprietors of many other kinds and sizes of businesses. However,
this month‟s discussion goes beyond ordinary “productivity,” to the idea of
meaningful
productivity; doing things that are critical, and in some cases,
uniquely critical to this type
of business. As preface, let me say – confess? – that I am old school on this. I built my
business with personal relationships, not e-mails and automated responses; and I continue
to do my part to perpetuate the business the same way. I have a different perspective (of
35 years) about this entire business than many who‟ve built theirs quickly, if in the 2003
to 2007 term, quite easily (
deceptively easily), and with heavy reliance on the internet.
Only you can decide for yourself if my perspective still matters. But here it is.
Things Info-Marketers Don‟t Give Time To,
That They Should
1: Reading „white mail.‟ I learned this from Halbert and had it reinforced by a few mail-order old-
timers, Cossman, Joffee. “White mail” means correspondence from customers, other than orders,
notably including complaints, but also their questions. This is a way to stay in touch and have a
sense of who your customers are. If they‟re literate, rational, childish, 101 or advanced, young or
old, etc. It is THE way to stay on top of customer service failures and customer dissatisfaction. If
you have staff handling, sorting and dealing with in-bounds, they‟ll hide problems from you, so
frequently and at random, you need to get to it first.
2: Responding to fan mail – presuming you get it (and if you don‟t, I think that should be
troubling). By fan mail, I don‟t just mean little notes of praise and thank-you; I mean the people
writing to tell you their stories, share their opinions, and relate to you on a personal level. These
people view their relationship with you as something meaningful and important, so an automated,
standard e-mail response is the equivalent of peeing on their shoes and spitting in their face. They
took time to communicate personally and they deserve a personal response. Mine can be as little
as a
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 16 --

handwritten note across whatever came from them or on a scratchpad page; as much as a lengthy,
multi-page response. But I answer it, and have for more than 30 years. One of the ways I judge my
work is how much of this I get, and its depth. Yes, this does sometimes breed pen-palitis to excess,
that must be gently curtailed. But on par, it‟s valuable. As an aside, I think it‟s very dangerous and
damaging to encourage communication with you that you know you can‟t or won‟t keep up with, or
permit the expectation of personal response and then deliver something less – this a great concern
of mine re. online social media. A friend recently told me of her horror at seeing an increasing
amount of rather vile, angry rage postings to her Facebook site and elsewhere, about her “being
too high „n mighty” to act appropriately in the online/Facebook culture; refusing some friends; not
personally communicating; sending some to a one way fan page as second class citizens. There is
push-back in this environment if you aren‟t willing to play by their rules. Uh-oh.
3: Investigation of the disaffected; prevention of „bad blood.‟ Not long ago, a client received a
cryptic farewell message from a departing coaching group member. He thought it odd and
delegated the task of following up with this exiting customer to a staff person. That staffperson
confused (as is so often the case) trying to connect with actually getting something done*, so one
message was left, and nothing more done. Until months later, this disgruntled customer published
rants all over the internet and filed regulatory complaints – all of which is baseless and will
eventually go away, but at considerable cost in time and, quite possibly, lawyers‟ Mercedes
payments. Could the mess have been avoided with much more proactive, persistent work at the
outset to reach out to this disaffected customer? There‟s no way of knowing in this specific
instance. But as general principle, I assure you, the answer is: yes.
(*As an aside, most folks do not seem to understand the difference between trying; putting in motion vs. certain
completion. If, for example, I tell you to make sure Harry knows about the Friday conference call and Friday comes
with Harry absent, and when I inquire, you say: “Well, I sent him an e-mail”, you don‟t get it at all. I didn‟t ask you to
send him an e-mail and thus discharge your responsibility without actually achieving the desired outcome; I explicitly
said: MAKE SURE Harry knows about the call. And even if I wasn‟t that explicit -- although I usually am, my intent was
clear. I could toss a note on a paper airplane out the window and hope all by myself, without involving you at all.
People need constant teaching and reminding about this; employees about what they‟re actually being paid for: actual
results. Interestingly, this problem dates back at least as far as the Civil War. It‟s the subject of a much reprinted essay
titled „Who Can Carry A Message To Garcia?‟ If somebody in your employ frequently refuses to get actual results, they
don‟t get what their job actually is, and they need to get gone! If YOU don‟t know the difference between doing and
actually done, my sympathies to all who depend on you.)
Disaffected customers who are with us for a while and then wander off are, of course, part and
parcel of our businesses, and most others. Have to shrug most of it off. But it is the departing
customer with a big beef, with a lot of anger or frustration or disappointment, who can prove
extremely costly. Early in my business life I was completely uninterested in soothing such folks,
and it didn‟t matter to me if they left mad or how mad they were when they left – if they‟re leaving,
who cares if merely muttering or cursing or screaming in rage? Gone is gone, and good riddance,
and “next.” Over time, I modified my thinking about this. And these days, when everybody has
equal footing to broadcast their angry rants and graffiti via the internet, this is a whole new
ballgame. Ask the CEO of Southwest Airlines if he‟d rather they had dealt with that guy with the
damaged guitar before he made his YouTube video that went viral, was viewed by millions, and
reported every place from CNN to The Tonight Show?
4: Event site selection and site planning. If you book facilities long-distance and/or use outside,
freelance event planners or companies, you need to remember that hardly anybody understands
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 16 --

our kind of meetings. Most conventions‟ operators, for example, couldn‟t care less who attends
sessions vs. who doesn‟t, instead choosing hallways, bar, night-life away from the site, etc. – but
we care deeply; we want them in the sessions; after hours, we want everybody easily found by
everybody else, ideally in one central bar – not split up in four or five. The size and shape and
lighting and entrances, exits and traffic flow all matter to us, not just aesthetically, but from the
standpoint of facilitating selling. I don‟t believe in delegating „place‟ any more than I would delegate
agenda. Further, I think you need to make the site “yours” during your event, not just hold the event
there. By that, I mean you want your attendees to have the experience of leaving their world and
the ordinary world and being in yours. Bill Glazer is an expert retailer, so our “store area” is always
smartly organized; merchandise labeled and displayed well, signage effective. You see us do
things like the DIAMOND Members‟ Lounge. Often at my own events, I have a Movie Night, in a
“theater” with red carpet entry and movie snacks and popcorn. I always strive to have a hotel or
wing of the hotel all to ourselves, without another entity‟s meetings and people in the same general
area.
5: Quality control. With size, detail is sacrificed. Some of this is absolutely unavoidable – and a
good trade in economic terms; some, though, is sacrifice to a false god of size at any cost, and that
is destructive. Too much, too often excused and defended, let slide to avoid the time and trouble of
the fight and weariness of trying to teach the same lesson; people who need fired and replaced,
remain. When too much going out your doors is, even in small ways, incongruent with what you
teach and stand for, a cancer has begun its work…initially, probably undiagnosed, and by the time
it makes itself evident, its work irreversible. I am far, far from infallible, but personally, I‟ve always
obsessed over and checked the details, and bitterly fought compromise of them. For many years,
while traveling incessantly, and before e-mail an option, I was getting faxes and FedEx's on arrival
at nearly every stop, to check the lay-outs of a cover for a manual or cassette album or a
newsletter or direct-mail piece, catch the bad line breaks, the type fonts changed, etc. and insist on
their repair; to proofread; to have my say about just about everything heading into customers‟
hands as a representative of me. I had my office phones mystery-shopped often. I made surprise
visits. I eavesdropped. I went in on weekends and snooped in desk drawers, stacks of work,
closets. In my work in the infomercial field, I rarely turned over the script I was paid to write without
going and being present during the shoot (for which I was often not paid and sometimes
unwelcome) – because I knew my work would be damaged at that stage if I abdicated all
watchdogging. This is the main reason I don‟t work in that industry now; my unwillingness to travel
to the west coast. I battle my book publishers over the places the editor chooses to break lines in
subheads and it gripes me mightily, the ones they fail to fix as I indicate: I teach that this matters,
how can I ignore it? So, here‟s a phrase for you: “congruent quality.” Whatever is “congruent
quality” for you MUST be treated as a big thing, even in the littlest of things. In a big business, if I
now had one, I‟d have a full-time Congruent Quality Snoop And Enforcer, thoroughly trained in
what the business was really all about, taught, represented; whose only job was prowling about,
catching people and output in act of incongruent quality.
On a personal note, in keeping with my wont for transparency and “No B.S.” disclosure: this has been and is the most
difficult thing for me as an author/content provider and personality with little or no control over much of what makes its
way into the marketplace representative of me. Every book published is end result of negotiation with editors and
publisher and they get final vote – the minute I take their advance, they own that content, I do not. With Glazer-
Kennedy Insider‟s Circle™, my name in its name should fool no one; I neither own any of the company or control
anything done there. Don‟t misunderstand: I am eternally grateful for the relationship, admiring of Bill‟s leadership and
success with the business, and sincerely hold it up as a „model citizen‟ in the info-marketing world for your study and
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 16 --

emulation. But. There certainly are things done, products produced, “stuff” that goes out perceived by the public as
representative of me that I do consider incongruent with what I believe and teach, and there are times I cringe at what I
see. To be 1000% candid, some similar compromises got made back when it was my business, and a smaller one –
out of economic necessity, time pressure, the need to let go of something. Most authors and creative folks feel the
same kind of conflicted emotions toward their masters; most novelists, for example, hate the movies made from their
books, but want the income and fame that can only come from signing over those rights and letting a group of people
take over and do what they will with the work. (Stallone refused to sell his original „Rocky‟ script unless he starred in
the movie and controlled its production, even though he was damn near starving at the time, and had responsibility of
wife and baby. Few of us have balls – or integrity – that big. I don‟t.) “Starving artists” have a word for the rest of us:
sell-outs. I‟ve starved and I didn‟t like it. When I have too raw a reaction to something done with my stuff or in my
name, I rub money on the wound. Point is, YOU (HAVE TO) CHOOSE. Only you can decide what you are willing to
shrug off, and what you‟re willing to draw lines in the sand over, whether in running your own business, or in handing
over content or products or customers or access to them, to others. Personally, I‟ve had six different book publishers,
and books put out by them and advertisements prepared by them, and marketing done by them; product produced by
five companies including Nightingale-Conant and marketing done by all of them; in various ways I‟ve been represented
in the marketplace by quite a few different people and entities, and it should be no surprise, I haven‟t been entirely
satisfied with any of them. But, I was rarely totally satisfied with what I put out, over which I at least theoretically had
total control, either. It seems to me that almost every day of my 35 years has been a battle to “get it right.” And day to
day, year to year, situation by situation, I consciously decide – and I believe that‟s the key – what needs fought to the
death over, what ought to slide on by. The thing you need to keep in mind, though, is too much incongruity, too much
noticeable incongruities will un-do what you painstakingly build, and will be the undetected secret reason for
slumping sales, diminishing responsiveness; diminished value of customers really representing diminished trust,
damaged brand. Be very conscious of this.
6: Staff training and coaching. Every place I‟ve had staff, I‟ve struggled, but fought hard to get
them “coached up” on what we were really doing….and I worked on attitudes, not just skills or
tasks. In my manufacturing business, I paid factory workers to actually listen to the audio products
we were producing and give me oral or written reports. I spent endless hours talking to my
salespeople, not about numbers, but about what we were about. My operating theory has always
been: if you can figure out how to achieve your goals without staff, by all means do so. But if you
are going to have humans, then accept the responsibility for controlling their performance.
What I see now, with many clients, is people with the right resume but no comprehension
whatsoever of the unique critical factors of our kinds of businesses, tossed into jobs they then do
very poorly in relationship to those critical factors, yet quite capably and correctly based on their
concept of the job, formed elsewhere. This is not a problem only in info-businesses. Happens in
dental practices. Restaurants. Everywhere. But it‟s a hyper-problem in our kinds of businesses. It is
easy in our kinds of businesses for staff (and sometimes, owners) to have disdain for the customer,
to view them negatively, openly criticize them, and this quickly turns into very repellant energy
sensed by customers. It‟s like a dog whistle only dogs can hear; it‟s a stench only customers can
smell. There is always a tendency to be impatient with their slow progress (by our standards), with
their neediness or questions. At least in a restaurant, people get food, eat it and feel full. Our
businesses; info-businesses are really all about intangibles. About ideas, and more so, about
feelings and relationships. Regardless of how technical the content is, we‟re all really in the
motivation business. If you and everybody on your staff aren‟t making the customers feel better
about themselves and about their relationship with you after every encounter and communication
than they did before, you‟re on thin ice.
7: Lagniappe creation. Look up the word and its origins, if you aren‟t familiar with it. In
„Unfinished Business,‟ I talk about the doughnut shop where, when we bought a dozen to take
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 16 --

home, they always gave me – the kid – a hot, fresh 13th one free, to eat on the way home. There is
also the lesson of the toy SURPRISE in the Crackerjack box. I am absolutely convinced that
„constant surprising‟ (which seems a contradiction but isn‟t) is vital to this business – and that
conversely, too much predictability (no matter its quality or value) is inevitably fatal, usually via slow
death.
8: Making yourself an informed, interesting, relatable human being people want to be with,
and feel not just respect but affection for. This is why Richard Nixon had to get on a helicopter
and get out of Washington D.C. just to avoid impeachment but Bill Clinton got away with debating
the meaning of the word “is,” defying impeachment, serving his entire term, and then making a
fortune on speaking tour and as corporate gadfly. Nobody liked Nixon. It‟s tough to admit you
aren‟t liked or likeable. Everybody has some friends. The joke is that even Hitler was kind to his
mother, had a hot looking girlfriend, and a loyal dog. But, if behind your back, your followers feel no
strong, binding, emotional personal connection to you, I think you‟re trouble waiting to happen.
Elvis made $50-million in 2009, turned 75 in January 2010. He‟s a $50-million a year entertainer all
these years after his death for reasons other than his music. George Foreman faced up to the fact
that he‟d succeeded as a world champion but been profoundly un-likeable and even more
profoundly un-liked throughout that career, so there were no opportunities for him after those peak
years. So he re-made his personality, returned to boxing and used it to merchandise the new
George Foreman, so, now, long after his gloves hang unused, lots of people want to be with him –
even if that only means having the countertop grill with his name on it in their kitchen. Such a
conscious self-analysis and deliberate re-making of self is very difficult, so very rare. But, again, it
is my contention that if behind your back, your followers feel no strong, binding, emotional personal
connection to you, I think you‟re trouble waiting to happen. And if you are unwilling to be and
present yourself as the kind of person that people have these positive feelings for, you need to
either exit this kind of business or, as quickly as you can, build such a big one, in a big enough
market that there‟s no end to customers to churn, that you can live entirely on transients, and
manage entirely by statistics. There are obviously any number of such “institutional” info-
businesses that work just fine, and if that‟s the sort of business you aspire to having, you need to
carefully study and model those. Frankly, that‟s not the kind of how-to‟s that I bring you.
You may think all this contrary to the way I relate to my herd. If so, it‟s not my intention you feel that
way. Since I first came to understand it, in the late 1970‟s, I have performed as – quoting Kirby
Landis – a “stern but loving parent.” I believe that to be the way I can be of greatest value to
people, and it‟s a role I‟ve been able to function in successfully. There are other roles you might
prefer that can also be serviceable in this field. But this at least requires conscious thought.
My made-known, made-famous inaccessibility, low tolerance for fools, impatience, etc., etc. serves
as dramatic contrast for “the softer side of Dan” – made known
more person to person albeit with hundreds in a year, Be somebody
thousands over time, and then by word-of-mouth they want at their
than by public broadcast. party, not the guy
they just feel
Then there is the matter of being interesting to people. obligated to invite.
I‟m amazed at info-marketers who think they can get by
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 16 --

being only narrowly, poorly informed, and uninteresting. You need life-experience stories. Enviable
adventures. To use a tired cliché, teachable moments. And you need to be a repository and bearer
of information, trivial as well as significant, more far-ranging than theirs. You need to be
entertaining. Somebody they really want at their party, not someone they feel obligated to invite.
This means reading – a lot; quantity, quality, variety, variety, variety. It means being tuned in to at
least major pop culture. You don‟t need to actually watch „the Wives of‟ shows, but you need to
know they exist. If it does not come naturally to you, it needs to be high priority work.
The Big Rocks vs. Little Rocks,
Stepping Over Dollars To Pick Up Dimes
and Don‟t Sweat The Small Stuff Theories
This has great validity, but not perfect validity, and therein lies the danger.
Yes, too many people major in minor matters, permit their time to be democratically consumed
regardless of each consumer‟s comparative, relative contribution to income, and fail to separate
the big stuff from the small stuff. Yes, there is even a lot of small stuff that can be procrastinated
over or ignored altogether with nominal harm. And yes, Ron LeGrand is right: a lot of people step
over dollars to pick up dimes. But the two implicit problems with this are correctly determining what
is small and what is big; and the fact that a tiny pebble can, in fact, make a big horse lame or even
de-rail a locomotive. There is also the fact that sanctioning little dysfunctions is guaranteed to lead
to bigger and bigger dysfunction.
Please re-read pages 229-230 of my No B.S. Ruthless Management book, for the million-dollar-
piece-of-gum story. This, from Disney, demonstrates the flaw in this don‟t-sweat-small-stuff theory
perfectly. And while you‟re at it, check Chapter 17 as well.
The “Gee, Mom, Everybody‟s Doing It”
Modus Operandi (Or Excuse)
The 12/09 issue of Book Marketing Up-Date (published by Steve and Bill Harrison/rtir.com)
featured a terrific article about Kelly Corrigan, who has made her book a blockbuster bestseller,
with 260,000 copies sold (!), beginning with a $5,000.00 promotional budget (!!), and utilizing a
YouTube video that has received 4.5 million hits. (Note: 4.5-million hits, only 260,000 books sold by
it + all other efforts.) Here is the thing I want to give her a big Hurrah! for… she says:
Don’t waste time blogging on the national bookstore chains’ web sites.
She blogged for Barnes & Noble and Powells. She didn‟t see sales from either, or from her
interview on BN.com, so she stopped. You have to understand, the pressure on an author, from
peers, publisher, retailers, etc., to do this – blog at such sites – is enormous, and when you resist,
you‟re considered an uncooperative idiot. The pressure on you from peers, associates, employees,
and countless others to waste your precious time on minimally productive, unproductive or outright
harmful activities are enormous. You must defy them.
Make THIS note:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 16 --

YOU AND YOU ALONE must determine what is productive/profitable
vs. what is a poor investment or outright waste of time, in your
business and in your life, and then you must act accordingly, regardless
of what resistance, pressure or criticism may come your way. Do not
permit yourself to waste time, to satisfy others’
opinions.
Please re-read that preceding paragraph. Maybe write it big „n bold on a wall poster. This doesn‟t
mean being closed-minded to new, different or better ideas – consider any and all that can be had
from qualified sources. But don‟t be bullied into doing anything just „cuz “everybody else” is doing it
or so-and-so is doing it or it‟s normal, customary and expected or „cuz it‟s cool and hip and cutting
edge, or for any other reason other than your own determination that it is productive, profitable or
otherwise right for you. To thine own self, be true.
I recently began writing a column for Entrepreneur.com, but I‟ve insisted on a definitively
measurable tracking mechanism for the new GKIC members it produces, and if it fails with that,
and there‟s no huge upswing in book sales that can be attributed to it, I‟ve told them, I‟ll quit. IT is
on 6 months‟ probation. In this, I am considered strange, stupid, unreasonable, and unappreciative.
They think they‟re giving me something I should be grateful for. I think „it‟ costs me about $2,000.00
per column in time, and I want a multiple of that, or I want to invest my time elsewhere. And I would
rather be thought of as strange, stupid, unreasonable and unappreciative than actually be stupid.
As you probably leapt ahead of me and thought, I direct these comments at social media, not
exclusively, but inclusively. It may seem everybody’s doing it. That‟s an epic lie, but even if it were
true, it still shouldn‟t dictate your behavior. Fully 90% of the U.S. population eats McDonald‟s once
a week or more, but if you‟re smart, you‟ll avoid the place like the plague.
Where Do You REALLY Make Your Money?
This is a very difficult thing to determine in this kind of business, because so much depends on the
relationship with the customer.
If you are running Zappos and selling shoes, you are in a much simpler business, and it is easier to
identify the most critical elements. Selection, inventory to support immediate shipment, customer
acquisition, customer service (in the traditional sense), brand promotion. Bill would tell you that, in
retail, what, when and how you buy is almost as important as selling. But if you are running an
information / membership business generally following my models, the critical elements are not
nearly so cut and dried.
For example, I think picking the right mix of Members to give recognition to each month in the
newsletter, in part, in order to suggest ascension (and not suggest anything else), as well as the
aforementioned personal responses to Members‟ mail or faxes – and encouragement of it – is a
critical factor. At some size, it would be entirely sacrificed, much like my friends who own a huge
number of Taco Bells tells me they manage entirely by statistics. But at the always-fighting-for-
your-life size most of our businesses stay, I think it‟s critical. Individuals matter, not just statistics.
People have handwritten notes from me framed, on their walls. You cannot delegate or automate
that. I recently got a card from Dr. Nielsen, recognizing our 25th anniversary, saying “can you
believe we’ve been together for 25 years?” Yes, I can, and loss of lifers distresses me. And I
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 16 --

honestly don‟t think you can sustain success in this, without having some “heart” for it. You can,
selling shoes. Not this. You can look very smart and rack up some big numbers for a few years,
but let‟s see you make it work for 35.
You need to determine for yourself where your money comes from and how your money is
actually made in your business - then tend to those things with the utmost care.
About two years ago, an info-marketer with a very successful newsletter business stopped his
printed edition and delivered only online, making subscribers come and fetch it when posted. I
cautioned against it, and I was dismissed as an antique. Just for clarity, if we could deliver
everything electronically and save the fortune spent on print and audio deliverables without
destroying our business, I‟d be the happiest guy on the planet. In his case, his subscriber base
quickly declined, and the responsiveness of those left fell off even more sharply. Today, his
business is a sad shadow of its former self. Problem: he thought his money was made by putting
together great information and ideas and selling it. He did understand being a provocative,
entertaining personality was important. He did not understand people receiving packages and
opening them with eager anticipation, and taking his newsletter with them to the can or the back
porch, and marking it up with hi-liters, and putting it in notebooks they saw on their bookshelves
was important.
You gotta constantly assess: what‟s really important?
No Perfect Horse
I suppose, over a dozen years, I‟ve purchased and owned or owned pieces of 300 horses, maybe
more. I have paid as little as $1,200.00, as much as $75,000.00. I have bred them, bought them as
yearlings and 2 year olds, and as mature racehorses, from 4 to 10. (Retirement age is 14). I‟ve had
a few superstars, including a 2 Year Old of the Year. A few great earners. Many mid-level earners
but relentless workers and true pros. Many disappointments. Everything in between. What I‟ve
never had is the perfect horse, entirely free of confirmation flaws, physical problems, medical
conditions, behavioral issues, mood swings, bouts of dysfunction. Not once. Not ever. And I don‟t
expect to. So it is with joint venture allies and individuals you might do business with, do deals with,
gain access to their customers or grant access to your customers – whether they‟ve got smaller or
bigger businesses than yours, been around longer than you or are less experienced. So it is with
people you might develop as authors, speakers, trainers, coaches. It isn‟t just that they all go lame;
hell, they all start out lame. But in racing, we learn that lameness is relative. There is life ending
lameness; career ending lameness; but mostly, manageable lameness.
DELUSION is
one of the
biggest
wastes of time
It‟s a WASTE OF TIME to look for, hope for
or insist on the perfect horse. If you are going to insist on
business relationships only with the non-lame (“sound”) individual
who perfectly fulfills your idea of smart, competent, capable,
co-operative, reliable, etc., you‟re going to waste a ton of time of all.
starting situations that go nowhere or, at best, hold together for
the briefest of times and probably end in acrimony. Of course, you can‟t tolerate high, toxic,
unredeemable levels of ignorance or stupidity, incompetence or unreliability. But, typically, part of
the price you pay for the benefits sought through another person in our industry is some level of
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 16 --

tolerance for their foibles. I believe in “nursing a situation along” if there appears to be sufficient
potential benefit. Maybe the person with the key to the door I want to pass through is an idiot, but
he has the key and I don‟t. A lot of blockheads have customers, influence, money.
When you are deciding, at the very beginning, whether or not to attempt doing “x” with someone,
consider what the time cost will be of managing that person and the relationship. If you are only
willing to invest the minimal amount of time you think should be required if dealing with somebody
who is entirely on the same page and is as incredibly smart and savvy as you are, knows what you
know, operates as you do and that you approve of, and can be relied on as you can, don’t start. I
have clients who‟ve created enormously lucrative situations by working co-operatively, but I can
point to many more who wound up bitterly frustrated with such attempts – and each other –
because of wholly unrealistic expectations about the other person.
Leverage: OPC
The single biggest consumption of time (and money) in most info-businesses is: getting new
customers. Many info-marketers are smart and adept at developing value in customers once
acquired, but handicapped and held back by the difficulty of obtaining them in the first place.
This is why anything you can figure out, to get connected directly with somebody else‟s good or
best customers (rather than finding them “needle in a haystack” style) is usually worth more time
and money than most info-marketers are willing to give it. This question of “what will you pay to
obtain a new customer?” (plus the smarter question: “How can you increase that maximum
allowable acquisition cost?”) has to be viewed in broader terms than just dollars paid for
advertising, but in money + time + trouble invested in working relationships with others who have
the customers you want. OPC, Other Peoples‟ Customers provide a lot of leverage.
You should expect to pay a price for it – not just in monetary terms, Tapping into
but in customizing rather than insisting on force-fitting OPC
every such opportunity into your single, standard “round hole”… isn’t
in creating…in “making nice with”…etc. supposed to be
easy or cheap.
I recently “pre-arranged” a truly ideal situation for one of my clients
with another, that, if done as I‟d suggested, would have provided
a permanent, continuous, albeit small feed of good new customers to Client #A, and a nice,
continuing, passive income to Client #B, without interfering with their other promotional activities.
Client #A then “downgraded” this to a one-shot reciprocal use of lists for single promotions, and
worse, “force-fit” his standard promotion (a poor fit for Client #B‟s customers). The result is certain
failure. A waste of time for them and me. And a mistake I won‟t make with Client #A again.
Get this: effectively, successfully taping into OPC isn‟t supposed to be easy or cheap. It‟s too
valuable for that.
Leverage: Get (Truly) Expert Help
Bill Glazer successfully put his OUTRAGEOUS Advertising That’s OUTRAGEOUSLY Successful
book on a number of bestseller lists last year, including USA TODAY, BUSINESS WEEK and, of
course, amazon. Something of a circus feat, believe me. To his credit, he not only invested time in
making a study of how this is done, but he invested money in securing advice, and in some cases,
services of bona-fide experts to make it happen. Without them, he‟d have spent more money in the
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 16 --

currency of his own time, and more than likely, gotten poorer results or failed altogether. You really
can tell whether somebody‟s serious about something, or delusional or blowing smoke, by their
investments (or lack thereof) in expert help. For example, I banged out a political novel, The Last
U.S. President’s Last Speech, last year that, if I say so myself, is damn good and proving itself
prescient, but I wasn‟t serious and am not serious about making much of it; it‟s only available as an
e-book (at www.Smashwords.com), and I have not invested in hiring experts to mount a YouTube
viral video campaign (which I know could make the book pop) or an appropriate literary agent to
shop it to publishers or a publicist, or any advisor with a track record of assisting authors of political
books. So let‟s not kid ourselves.
Yes, I‟m being self-serving in urging the securing of leverage by getting expert help; I make my
living selling days of consulting, on-going private client consulting/coaching featuring one-on-one
monthly calls, and copywriting. I am not a cheap date either. I am amazed at some clients, which
report dramatic financial rewards and speed and reduction or elimination of experimentation from
my advice, who then disconnect or fail to at least periodically purchase it. I respect the client who
has something flop I‟ve worked on, but who persists with me, and winds up with a big success. I‟m
certain different clients think differently about what they are buying from me. The dumbest think
that they‟re buying sales letters or time. The smartest ones are buying leverage. As example, I
recently had an on-again, off-again client fly seven people from the west coast to meet with me for
under 5 hours in Cleveland. My fee, plus juice for their jet, plus the catered lunch, at least
$35,000.00, not to mention the time cost of everybody‟s day away from work. Way too much for
advice. A bargain for leverage. Personally, in my consulting/copywriting practice, I frankly noticed
a slowing of new, fresh inquiries started about mid-2009 and continues, undoubtedly bred by
recession-response reluctance to spend money. Fortunately, I‟m not financially affected by it; I
don‟t need to work. But, if it‟s more critical than ever not to waste money or time, and to get the
best results possible from every move, isn‟t it smarter than ever to buy leverage?
Leverage: Size And Scope of Opportunity
I‟ve often ruminated and commented about – skills that are a 10 on 1-10, applied to opportunities
that are 4‟s and 5‟s. It‟s a dangerous trap. On the other hand, taking attention away from the core
business that has been supporting you – when there‟s still plenty of unexploited opportunity in it, to
chase unicorns somewhere over the rainbow, equally dangerous. Within the core business, each
thing that might be improved has to be weighed against others, based on likely contribution to net
income, if best conceivable results are achieved. For example, info-marketers often obsess over
their refunds, but if they are relatively low and, with extreme effort, might only be lowered by 1% or
2%, we have to know (a) what that 2% represents in money, to judge the size and scope of this
opportunity, and (b) compare that potential income to the potential income of other opportunities in
the same business. We shouldn‟t just decide to work on it because it‟s there.
Leverage: CAREFUL „Partnering‟
As noted above, they all start out a little lame. All go lame. CAREFUL is a very important word
here. But doing it all yourself is slow and tedious.
I have, for example, begun – late in my writing career – doing books with full-fledged co-authors
rather than alone. The first was Uncensored Sales Strategies with Sydney Barrows. I‟m just
finishing one with Chip Kessler, about “the lost secrets of Dr. Brinkley‟s marketing.” That one offers
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 16 --

insight: Chip brought research, invested time in working back and forth with the Kansas Historical
Society and the Brinkley family to acquire archive materials, and wrote a draft of the book. Without
him, it would have consumed, over time, weeks and weeks of my time; with him, I got my parts
done in 3.5 days, total. For this reason, for the first time ever, I‟m partnering with a co-author on my
next book in the No B.S. series, this one about price strategies. This loops back to knowing where
you really make your money. As author of books, the income from the royalties is small in
comparison to the income that filters to me from the Glazer-Kennedy Members and/or product
buyers brought in by the books and by special promotions built around the release of each new
book. Giving away a hunk of the royalties in exchange for slashing the time investment in getting a
good book researched, written and into the marketplace is a great trade, because I don‟t make my
money by writing books; I make my money by having books published, promoted and in the
marketplace. In a very real sense, I‟m wasting time doing all or the bulk of the writing myself, and
I‟m not supposed to be wasting time!
No, you may not write books for mainstream publication. But you have to ask yourself what you do
– or want to do, that would be profitable directly or indirectly, that you don‟t – that could be made
easier or faster or less tedious; that you could be more prolific about; if you involved someone else
or others in the “production.” (My popular POWER POINTS product published by Glazer-Kennedy
exists because I had Kathy Tolleson do all the grunt work for it, and I polished. The original
RENEGADE MILLIONAIRE SYSTEM exists because Lee Milteer did the drudgery of culling
questions and key points from all my previously written, recorded and published works. I doubt
either of these would ever have moved off my “C” list if I had decided to do the work myself.)
You have to ask yourself what you could be more prolific about, if you had someone else do the
marketing….or run the operation…..or conduct the trainings….or, or, or, or.
Well, there you have it, a rambling diatribe on how info-marketers use or mis-use time (and
other resources), and an insider‟s look at much of my one-on-one, on-going conversations
(and arguments) with myself about the subject. It may be the most important subject to
think, think, think and think more about, because time seems to be THE only resource none
of us can ever get enough of. Most of us have enough food, water and shelter, unlike folks in
most of the world and hunks of the United States. Most of us have enough brains to come in
out of the rain, unlike the current occupants of Congress and the White House. It‟s possible
to have enough health and energy, and many do. It‟s definitely possible to have enough
money, at least from a practical standpoint, if not an emotional one. But nobody seems to
ever have enough time, do they? So, I hope you agree this thinking out loud on the subject
is worthwhile, and that you are provoked by it to think carefully about your own
investments of time as a result.
www.getwsodownload.com

www.getwsodownload.com
